content.content:
content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Where is my package? Tracking\
  \ Status Help
- url: https://faq.usps.com/articles/FAQ/Where-is-my-package
- source:\
  \ United States Postal Service
- domain: faq.usps.com

## Content

# Where\
  \ is my package? Tracking Status Help

USPS uses many package-tracking scans.\
  \ Each status describes a different point in the delivery lifecycle, and the next\
  \ step depends on the actual status shown in tracking. Customers can check the latest\
  \ tracking result for the package and use that status to understand what USPS is\
  \ currently doing.

## Delivered

A “Delivered” status means USPS\
  \ recorded a delivery date, time, and location. If the package cannot be found,\
  \ USPS recommends checking places where a carrier may have left it, including:
\
  
- a garage;
- other people in the household;
- the mailbox;
- a porch;
- weather-protected\
  \ areas;
- places out of street view;
- exterior doors and other possible drop-off\
  \ locations.

For apartment or condominium buildings, the package may have been\
  \ left with the management office. In a multi-unit community mailbox, a key in the\
  \ regular mail compartment may indicate that the package is in a parcel locker.
\
  
If the package still cannot be found after more than one postal business day from\
  \ the delivered scan, USPS directs eligible customers to use its service-request\
  \ process for the local Post Office.

## In Transit

“In Transit,”\
  \ “Arrived at USPS Facility,” and “Departed USPS Facility” indicate\
  \ that the package is being processed or transported toward the delivering Post\
  \ Office. Similar scans can appear more than once as the item moves through the\
  \ network.

USPS says the package is still on the way. Customers can follow later\
  \ tracking updates as the item reaches the delivery unit and moves to an out-for-delivery\
  \ status.

## Out for Delivery

“Out for Delivery” means the package\
  \ was prepared at the local Post Office and is expected to be delivered by a carrier\
  \ that day. Packages do not always arrive with regular letter mail and can be delivered\
  \ at a different time.

If the package has not been delivered after one full postal\
  \ business day following that scan, USPS provides a service-request path for eligible\
  \ items.

## No Access to Delivery Location

This status means the carrier could\
  \ not reach the mailbox, porch, garage, or other delivery location. USPS gives examples\
  \ such as a blocked mailbox, an animal, a locked or gated building, utility work,\
  \ or emergency activity.

For this status, USPS says no action is needed immediately\
  \ because another delivery attempt is normally made on the next business day. Customers\
  \ can check the area for anything blocking access.

## Available for Pickup

\
  “Available for Pickup” means the package cannot be delivered and is being\
  \ held at the local Post Office for pickup during business hours. USPS states that\
  \ Redelivery cannot be scheduled for a package in this status.

The customer or\
  \ an authorized agent can pick up the package, subject to the applicable identification\
  \ requirements and holding period.

These status descriptions show why a report\
  \ such as “my package did not arrive” is not, by itself, enough to identify\
  \ the correct process. The current tracking event determines which USPS guidance\
  \ is relevant.
"
metadata: {}
title: Where is my package? Tracking Status Help
url: https://faq.usps.com/articles/FAQ/Where-is-my-package

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Redelivery - The Basics
- url:\
  \ https://faq.usps.com/articles/Knowledge/Redelivery-The-Basics
- source: United\
  \ States Postal Service
- domain: faq.usps.com

## Content

# Redelivery -\
  \ The Basics

USPS Redelivery is the process used to get a mailpiece or package\
  \ delivered again after the first delivery attempt was not successful. It takes\
  \ place after an initial delivery attempt. USPS Delivery Instructions, by contrast,\
  \ are used before a delivery attempt to request eligible delivery options.

##\
  \ Checking Whether an Item Is Eligible

A customer may schedule Redelivery without\
  \ having a PS Form 3849 notice, but a tracking number or barcode number is needed\
  \ for online scheduling. Customers can check the item in USPS Tracking.

Only\
  \ packages with certain tracking scan events are eligible for Redelivery. The eligible\
  \ events listed by USPS include:

- Available for Redelivery or Pickup
- Notice\
  \ Left
- Receptacle Blocked
- Receptacle Full or Item Oversized
- No Secure Location\
  \ Available
- No Authorized Recipient Available
- Reminder to Schedule Redelivery
\
  - Available for Pickup, where the tracking result still allows the applicable customer\
  \ option

If the item does not show an eligible scan event, it cannot be scheduled\
  \ for Redelivery.

When USPS Tracking is used, a Redelivery option appears under\
  \ “Available Actions” only when the mail item is eligible for the service.
\
  
## Starting a Redelivery Request

Customers can begin online through the USPS\
  \ Redelivery service or from USPS Tracking. The tracking number from the package\
  \ or from a PS Form 3849 notice can be used to start the request.

For same-day\
  \ Redelivery, USPS states that the request must be submitted by 2 a.m. Central Time,\
  \ Monday through Saturday. Requests submitted after that cutoff move to a later\
  \ available day.

After the request is accepted, the confirmation page shows a\
  \ confirmation number. Customers should keep that number because it is needed to\
  \ review or change an existing request.

## When Redelivery Cannot Be Scheduled
\
  
USPS lists several conditions that can prevent a Redelivery request. Examples\
  \ include:

- the address entered does not match the original delivery address;
\
  - a Redelivery request already exists for the package;
- the package has already\
  \ been returned to the sender;
- the package is refused;
- the package has already\
  \ been delivered; or
- the package is already out for Redelivery.

There is a\
  \ maximum of one Redelivery request for a package.

## Related Redelivery Tracking\
  \ Events

USPS explains several tracking events associated with this process:
\
  
- **Available for Redelivery or Pickup:** the item can be picked up at the local\
  \ Post Office, or the customer can schedule Redelivery when the service is available.
\
  - **Prepared for Redelivery:** USPS is preparing the item for the requested delivery\
  \ date.
- **Out for Redelivery:** the item is currently out for the requested Redelivery\
  \ attempt.

USPS also notes that some mail requiring a signature or other accountable\
  \ service must be accepted by the addressee or an authorized agent. The item can\
  \ only be redelivered to the original address shown on the mailpiece.
"
metadata: {}
title: Redelivery - The Basics
url: https://faq.usps.com/articles/Knowledge/Redelivery-The-Basics

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Missing Mail and Lost Packages
\
  - url: https://www.usps.com/help/missing-mail.htm
- source: United States Postal\
  \ Service
- domain: usps.com

## Content

# Find Missing Mail

USPS provides\
  \ a Missing Mail process for mail or packages that may be lost or delayed. Before\
  \ beginning a search, USPS tells customers to check the current tracking status\
  \ when the item has tracking.

## Step 1: Check the Current Status

The first\
  \ step is to use USPS Tracking to see the item’s current status. Tracking may\
  \ show that the package is still moving through the network, has reached a delivery\
  \ stage, or has another recorded event that affects what should happen next.

\
  For Informed Delivery users, USPS notes that the digital preview of incoming mail\
  \ does not guarantee that every item will arrive on the same day it appears in the\
  \ preview.

## Step 2: Submit a Missing Mail Search Request

USPS says a Missing\
  \ Mail search request can be submitted starting seven days after mailing.

The\
  \ request asks for information that can help identify and locate the mailpiece,\
  \ including:

- the sender’s mailing address;
- the recipient’s mailing\
  \ address;
- the size and type of the container or envelope;
- identifying information\
  \ such as the tracking number, mailing date, mailing receipt, or Click-N-Ship label\
  \ receipt;
- a description of the contents, including details such as brand, model,\
  \ color, or size when relevant; and
- pictures that could help USPS recognize the\
  \ item.

Customers submit the search through the USPS Missing Mail system.

\
  ## What Happens After a Search Request

After USPS receives the request, it sends\
  \ a confirmation and provides periodic updates. If the package or mail is found,\
  \ USPS sends it to the address provided in the request when the item can be forwarded.
\
  
USPS also explains that not every missing item can be recovered. Some items may\
  \ not be found, and some recovered items cannot be forwarded because of safety restrictions.
\
  
## Refunds and Claims

A Missing Mail search is separate from other remedies.
\
  
Customers who used a service with a money-back guarantee, such as an eligible\
  \ Priority Mail Express service, may be able to request a refund under the applicable\
  \ rules.

For insured mail or packages, a customer may also be able to file an\
  \ insurance claim. USPS recommends filing promptly for damaged items or missing\
  \ contents and states that those claims have filing deadlines.

The Missing Mail\
  \ page is intended for items that may actually be lost or delayed. Its first instruction\
  \ is still to check the current status before starting the search process.
"
metadata: {}
title: Missing Mail and Lost Packages
url: https://www.usps.com/help/missing-mail.htm

Your package does **not currently appear to be lost**. USPS shows:

- **Status:** Delivery exception — *No Access to Delivery Location*
- **Last scan:** Los Angeles, CA 90017, **July 5, 2026 at 3:42 PM PDT**
- **Delivery attempts:** 1
- **Current stage:** Last-mile delivery
- **Return to sender:** Not started

This usually means the carrier couldn’t reach the mailbox or delivery location—for example, because of a locked gate, blocked mailbox, animal, or building access issue. USPS says another attempt is normally made on the **next business day**, and no immediate action is generally required.

### What to do next

1. **Check for anything blocking access** to your mailbox, porch, gate, or building entrance.
2. **Watch tracking for the next delivery attempt** on the next postal business day.
3. If USPS shows a **Redelivery** option, you can schedule it using the tracking number through USPS Tracking/Redelivery.
4. If there is no progress after the next attempt—or the package is still undelivered after roughly one postal business day following a delivery-stage scan—contact the local Post Office or submit a USPS service request.
5. A **Missing Mail search** is generally appropriate only if the package becomes delayed or appears genuinely lost; USPS says such a request can be submitted starting **seven days after mailing**.

Sources: [USPS Tracking Status Help](https://faq.usps.com/articles/FAQ/Where-is-my-package), [USPS Redelivery](https://faq.usps.com/articles/Knowledge/Redelivery-The-Basics), [USPS Missing Mail](https://www.usps.com/help/missing-mail.htm).

- **Status:** Delivery exception — *No Access to Delivery Location*
- **Last scan:** Los Angeles, CA 90017, **July 5, 2026 at 3:42 PM PDT**
- **Delivery attempts:** 1
- **Current stage:** Last-mile delivery
- **Return to sender:** Not started

This usually means the carrier couldn’t reach the mailbox or delivery location—for example, because of a locked gate, blocked mailbox, animal, or building access issue. USPS says another attempt is normally made on the **next business day**, and no immediate action is generally required.

### What to do next

1. **Check for anything blocking access** to your mailbox, porch, gate, or building entrance.
2. **Watch tracking for the next delivery attempt** on the next postal business day.
3. If USPS shows a **Redelivery** option, you can schedule it using the tracking number through USPS Tracking/Redelivery.
4. If there is no progress after the next attempt—or the package is still undelivered after roughly one postal business day following a delivery-stage scan—contact the local Post Office or submit a USPS service request.
5. A **Missing Mail search** is generally appropriate only if the package becomes delayed or appears genuinely lost; USPS says such a request can be submitted starting **seven days after mailing**.

Sources: [USPS Tracking Status Help](https://faq.usps.com/articles/FAQ/Where-is-my-package), [USPS Redelivery](https://faq.usps.com/articles/Knowledge/Redelivery-The-Basics), [USPS Missing Mail](https://www.usps.com/help/missing-mail.htm).
